file_name: Kaneko et al. - 2025 - Machine learning-based complementary artificial intelligence model for dermoscopic diagnosis of pigm.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used Google Cloud Platform's Vertex AI (AutoML) to train a supervised machine learning model for the classification of pigmented skin lesions (basal cell carcinoma, malignant melanoma, nevocellular nevus, and seborrheic keratosis) from dermoscopic images. The model achieved an average accuracy of 86.3% and precision of 87.3%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Article' and an 'experimental diagnostic study'. It reports original results from collecting 400 dermoscopic images, training a machine learning model using Google Cloud Platform's Vertex AI, and evaluating its performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) on pigmented skin lesions.

title: Machine Learning-based Complementary Artificial Intelligence Model for Dermoscopic Diagnosis of Pigmented Skin Lesions in Resource-limited Settings
brief_summary: This study evaluated the potential of free, accessible artificial intelligence tools for diagnosing pigmented skin lesions in resource-limited settings. The researchers collected 400 dermoscopic images from four pathological categories (basal cell carcinoma, malignant melanoma, nevocellular nevus, and seborrheic keratosis) and trained a supervised machine learning model using Google Cloud Platform's Vertex AI AutoML. The dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets in an 8:1:1 ratio, with performance assessed via confusion matrices and precision-recall curves. The model achieved an average accuracy of 86.3%, precision of 87.3%, recall of 86.3%, and an F1 score of 0.87 on the test set. Testing on separate, unseen cases yielded an accuracy of approximately 70%. The authors concluded that free AI tools can accurately classify pigmented skin lesions with minimal expertise, offering high-precision diagnostic support where dermatologists are scarce.

all_task_types.target: Pigmented skin lesion categories (basal cell carcinoma, malignant melanoma, nevocellular nevus, and seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The study trained a supervised machine learning model using Google Cloud Platform's Vertex AI AutoML to classify dermoscopic images into one of four discrete pathological diagnostic categories. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and confusion matrices for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: Institutional Dermoscopic Image Cohort
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of 400 dermoscopic images collected from patients with pigmented skin diseases who visited the authors' institution (Nippon Medical School Musashi Kosugi Hospital and Nippon Medical School Hospital). The images were labeled based on pathologically confirmed diagnoses. The data is not publicly available due to privacy concerns.

use.validation: Used for model validation during training (40 images).
use.testing: Used for evaluating model performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) on the held-out test set (40 images).
use.other: A separate subset of unseen cases from the same institution was used for external validation/testing, yielding an accuracy of approximately 70%.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, confusion matrices, and precision-recall curves.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Intentionally refrained from applying these methods during training to explore the feasibility of developing diagnostic models using freely available AI tools without programming expertise.

conclusions: Free AI tools, such as Google Cloud Platform's Vertex AI and AutoML, can be used to develop high-accuracy diagnostic models for pigmented skin lesions without requiring advanced programming or AI expertise.
The AI model achieved an average accuracy of 86.3%, precision of 87.3%, recall of 86.3%, and an F1 score of 0.87, demonstrating high diagnostic potential.
The model's accuracy was 100% for basal cell carcinoma and seborrheic keratosis, and 80% for malignant melanoma in the primary test set.
Testing on separate, unseen cases yielded an accuracy of approximately 70%.
Model accuracy improved with more training data but plateaued around 50 images per disease category.
The study suggests that accessible AI tools can provide high-precision diagnostic support in resource-limited settings or areas lacking dermatologists.
A key limitation is that the AI selected diagnoses from only four predefined options, whereas real clinical practice involves a wider range of differential diagnoses.

The study intentionally refrained from using data augmentation techniques (such as image inversion and rotation) to test the feasibility of using free tools with minimal expertise; future work plans to incorporate these techniques and expand sample sizes through multi-institutional collaboration.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small, single-institution dataset of only 400 images (100 per class), which may limit generalizability.
The AI model was restricted to a closed set of only four diagnostic categories, whereas real clinical practice involves a much wider differential diagnosis.
Data augmentation techniques (e.g., flipping, rotation) were intentionally omitted to test feasibility with minimal expertise, potentially underestimating model performance.
The dataset is not publicly available due to privacy concerns, preventing independent external validation.
Performance on separate, unseen cases from the same institution dropped to approximately 70% accuracy, indicating potential overfitting or sensitivity to data distribution shifts.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics.
The 'black box' nature of the AutoML model was acknowledged as a barrier to trust, with no explainability methods (e.g., Grad-CAM) applied in this study.
The study was approved by the ethics committee (approval no. 797-6-26) of Nippon Medical School.
